title: The Healing of the Leper
subtitle: Christ comes down from the mountain and stretches out his hand to touch what the Law cast out

stillness: Be still, and let the sermon on the mountain fall silent in you.
He who taught now comes down to confirm his words with deeds.
Wait here, where the road runs between the mountain and the city.
gospel: When Jesus had come down from the mountain,
and kneeling down, he fell upon his face,
"Lord, if you will, you can make me clean."
stretched out his hand and touched him,
And immediately his leprosy was cleansed.
behold: Behold the Lord Jesus descending from the mountain of divine excellence
into the valley of humility, into the lowliness of our flesh,
keeping what he was, taking up what he was not.
For he came down from the mountain when he emptied himself,
taking the form of a servant.
And see: a leper comes to him,
not so much with the steps of his body as with the faith of his heart.
With bent knee he falls forward, flat upon his face,
not hiding his stains, but humbly confessing and laying them bare.
He shows the wound; he asks for the remedy.
Behold the hand of Christ stretched out,
and watch it touch the rotting flesh the Law had driven from the camp.
consider: Three things meet here in the leper: faith, humility, prayer —
and to these three, nothing is denied by God.
Because he fell upon his face, he shows shame and lowliness;
so let each one blush at the stains of his own life.
Yet shame did not silence his confession:
he uncovered the wound, he begged the cure.
He did not say "heal me," but "if you will, you can" —
leaving the whole to God's will, as to the Almighty.
So learn not to demand cures for the body,
but to commit everything to the divine good pleasure,
who knows what is fitting and orders all things by his judgment.
Now mark the touch. Christ stretched out his hand —
this was generosity, against the greedy.
He touched him — this was humility, against the proud.
He said "I will" — this was tenderness, against the envious.
He said "be made clean" — this was power, against the unbelieving.
The leper whom the Law cast out beyond the camp,
whom scribe and Pharisee scarcely deigned to look upon,
him he did not refuse to touch —
that no one should spurn or shrink from a man
for any stain or sickness of the body.
And the leprosy that once defiled whoever touched it
now flees at the touch of the Lord, and departs.
prayer: who came down from the mountain of the Father's throne
to heal the leprosy of the human race:
sprinkled with the many stains of my sins.
If you will, you can make me clean.
Stretch out the hand of your grace and tenderness;
touch the inward and the outward of the leper who calls upon you;
have mercy on me, a penitent,
and command the sickness of sin to depart.
who do not will the death of the sinner,
but that he be converted and live:
accomplish this in me, a sinner,
that I may not die in my soul,
but be converted, and live with you forever. Amen.

latinJewel.latin: Ecce quidam leprosus veniens, non tam passibus corporis, quam fide cordis, genu flexo, procidens in faciem suam, adorabat eum.
latinJewel.english: Behold, a certain leper came, not so much with the steps of his body as with the faith of his heart, and with bent knee, falling upon his face, he adored him.
